title: Cursor Configuration
description: Generate rule files, ignore patterns, and indexing config for Cursor AI editor.

steps.title: Cursor Rule Files (.cursor/rules/)
steps.description: Configure multiple .mdc rule files under .cursor/rules/. Use focused files for security, testing, language/framework guidance, and manual workflows.
steps.hint: Cursor rules are committed to version control and used to shape context and behavior. Keep each rule file narrow and precise to reduce prompt bloat and improve relevance.

fields.label: Cursor Rules
fields.description: Define one or more Cursor rule files, each with its own filename, metadata, and instructions.

fields.label: Rule file name
fields.description: Filename relative to .cursor/rules/, for example security.mdc or python.mdc.

fields.label: Rule type
fields.description: Label the rule by its primary concern so the generated rules directory stays maintainable.

fields.label: Activation mode
fields.description: Specify how Cursor should activate this rule.
fields.screen_hint: Choose Always-on only for truly universal rules. Use manual-only for heavyweight guidance that should only be invoked explicitly.

fields.label: Rule description
fields.description: Short frontmatter description for this rule. Useful for semantic/manual rules and for Cursor's rule chooser.
fields.placeholder: Security best practices for backend services

fields.label: Applies To (Glob Patterns)
fields.description: Comma-separated glob patterns. Required for file-matched rules; optional otherwise.

fields.label: Always Apply
fields.description: Inject this rule into every Cursor context regardless of the active file. Use only for universal rules.

fields.label: Rule Instructions
fields.description: The guidance for this specific rule file. Keep content narrow and avoid mixing concerns across security, testing, and workflow guidance.
fields.placeholder: Add focused rules for this file...
fields.screen_hint: Use separate files for security, tests, language/framework guidance, and manual workflows. Narrow rules improve relevance and reduce context bloat.

steps.title: Agent Instructions (AGENTS.md)
steps.description: Configure AGENTS.md for Cursor agent guidance and cross-tool workflow documentation.
steps.hint: Cursor supports both Rules and AGENTS.md. Use AGENTS.md for broader agent workflow guidance, review processes, and reusable agent behavior expectations.

fields.label: Agent Guidance
fields.description: Rules and expectations for agentic workflows, review processes, and cross-tool behavior.
fields.placeholder:
# Agent guidance
- Use the security reviewer for security-critical changes.
- Use the testing agent for new or changed code needing coverage.
fields.screen_hint: Keep AGENTS.md focused on agent behavior and workflow guidance rather than general coding standards.

steps.title: Cursor Ignore File (.cursorignore)
steps.description: Control which files Cursor can access and use for AI features and context. This is a primary repo-level exclusion control, not an absolute enforcement boundary.
steps.hint: Committed .cursorignore patterns are shared with all team members. Add secrets, private keys, and sensitive artifacts here. Note that indexing behavior may also be affected by .gitignore.

fields.label: Ignore Patterns
fields.description: One pattern per line. Matches gitignore syntax. Use ! to re-include.
fields.screen_hint: Security-critical patterns (secrets, credentials) are always included. Add additional paths relevant to your project.
fields.locked_value:
# Secrets and credentials — primary exclusions
.env
.env.*
secrets/
*.pem
*.key
*.p12
*.pfx
*.jks
.aws/
.ssh/
.docker/config.json
fields.default:
# Build outputs
dist/
build/
.next/
__pycache__/
bin/
obj/

# Dependencies
node_modules/
.venv/
venv/
.cargo/

# IDE
.idea/
.vscode/settings.json
*.swp

steps.title: Cursor Indexing Ignore (.cursorindexingignore)
steps.description: Control which files Cursor indexes for AI context and search. Excluded files remain accessible but are not included in automatic indexing.
steps.hint: Large dependency directories and build output significantly impact indexing speed and relevance. Select an indexing strategy that matches your repository's tradeoffs. Note that .gitignore interaction can also affect indexing.

fields.label: Indexing strategy
fields.description: Choose how aggressively Cursor should exclude files from indexing.
fields.screen_hint: Balanced is recommended for most repos. Aggressive exclusions are useful only when indexing noise is a severe problem. Broad indexing is useful when test and generated files are important context.

fields.label: Indexing Ignore Patterns
fields.description: One pattern per line. Gitignore syntax. Matching files won't be indexed but remain accessible.
fields.screen_hint: Do not exclude tests by default — tests are often valuable context. Use test-specific exclusions only as an advanced opt-in.
fields.default:
# Large directories that slow indexing
node_modules/
.next/
dist/
build/
__pycache__/
.venv/

# Logs
*.log
logs/

presets.label: Tests & coverage (advanced opt-in)
presets.description: Test files are valuable context; use only if you need aggressive exclusions.

steps.title: Ignore Verification Checklist
steps.description: Generate a short checklist to help verify ignore-file effectiveness and indexing behavior.
steps.hint: Cursor ignore files are a primary exclusion control, but actual indexing behavior can depend on .gitignore and project settings. Validate by checking indexing counts and sample file visibility.

fields.label: Verification Checklist
fields.description: A short set of verification steps to confirm ignore and indexing behavior.
fields.default:
- Confirm .cursorignore contains secrets, private keys, and sensitive artifacts.
- Confirm .cursorindexingignore excludes large build and dependency directories.
- Verify .gitignore interaction for files that should not be indexed.
- Check Cursor indexing counts or workspace reports after updating ignore files.
- Open a known excluded file and confirm it is not included in automatic AI context.
- For manual-only rules, verify that they can be invoked explicitly by name.
